planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in partial shade with well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil; Hippeastrum papilio is evergreen — it does not go dormant and should not be allowed to dry out completely between waterings. Unlike most Hippeastrum species and hybrids that require a dry dormancy period to rebloom, H. papilio can bloom multiple times per year in good conditions without a rest period. Plant bulbs with the top third above soil level. In zone 9, container culture provides flexibility. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in partial shade to bright indirect light — Butterfly Amaryllis is significantly more shade-tolerant than most Hippeastrum species and hybrids, and actually performs better in partial shade than full sun in zone 9. Hippeastrum papilio is a truly unusual species within the large Hippeastrum genus — one of the most distinct and ornamentally striking species, native to a limited area of southern Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina states) in the Amaryllidaceae family. The flowers are the defining feature: large (6–8 inch), 6-petaled, pale green to yellow-white with bold, dark red-maroon to burgundy veining and striping on each petal, creating a pattern of vivid dark streaks on a pale background that strongly resembles a butterfly's wing — a resemblance that inspired the species name 'papilio' (Latin for butterfly). Unlike the brightly colored, cultivated Hippeastrum hybrids that go fully dormant in winter, H. papilio is an evergreen species that maintains its strap-like leaves year-round and can bloom multiple times per year without a dormancy period. The flowers appear in clusters of 2–4 at the top of tall scapes. The species has become an important parent in Hippeastrum hybridization, contributing its evergreen quality and unusual striped coloring to offspring. All parts are toxic due to lycorine and other alkaloids.
sources: American Hippeastrum Society — Hippeastrum papilio species notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Hippeastrum papilio profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hippeastrum species for Florida
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hippeastrum (toxic)

common_name: Amazon Lily (Eucharis Lily, White Amazon Lily, Madonna Lily of the Tropics)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil or potting mix; Amazon Lily cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 50°F. Unlike most bulbs, it prefers consistent moisture year-round and only needs a brief 4–6 week dry spell to trigger blooming. Grow in groupings for best display — single bulbs rarely bloom as freely as established clumps. In zone 9, container culture allows flexibility. Keep the bulb top at soil level. Propagate by separating offsets. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Amazon Lily is one of the best bulbs for shaded positions in zone 10-11 outdoor tropical gardens, producing its most beautiful and abundant flowers in the bright filtered shade under large trees. Eucharis amazonica (formerly and sometimes still listed as E. grandiflora) is a large-bulbed, evergreen geophyte from the humid tropical forests of Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia in the Amaryllidaceae family, producing some of the most beautiful and fragrant flowers available from a shade-tolerant tropical bulb. The flowers are pendant, large (3–4 inch), pure white, 6-petaled, with a prominent green and white central corona surrounded by six outward-spreading tepals — the combination of pendulous form, snowy white color, and green-tinged central cup creates a flower of exceptional elegance. The fragrance is intensely sweet, jasmine-like, and far-carrying. Flowers are produced in clusters of 3–6 at the top of tall scapes, typically in spring and fall after a brief dry rest period, and the blooming of an established clump with multiple simultaneous flower spikes is one of the finest flower displays available from a shade garden bulb. The large, broad, glossy, dark-green leaves (12–16 inches long) are attractive in themselves year-round. Amazon Lily is used as a cut flower and for lei making throughout the Pacific. All parts are toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Eucharis amazonica (Amazon Lily)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Eucharis lily cultivation notes
Royal Horticultural Society — Eucharis grandiflora cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Eucharis (toxic to pets)

common_name: Velvet Apple (Mabolo, Butter Fruit, Kamagong Fruit Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun to partial shade with well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Velvet Apple is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting. In zone 9, container culture is practical for young trees. Trees are dioecious — plant male and female trees for fruit production. Fruits have a peculiar strong cheese-like or onion-like odor when just picked that dissipates as the flesh ripens fully; the mature flesh itself has a sweet, mild, mango-banana flavor. Considered a luxury fruit in Filipino markets.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Velvet Apple grows most vigorously in full sun but tolerates and often benefits from afternoon shade protection in the intense heat of zone 9-10 summers. Diospyros blancoi is an evergreen fruit tree from the Philippines in the family Ebenaceae (the ebony and persimmon family), producing one of the most striking and unusual fruits in the tropical fruit world. The ripe fruits are perfectly round (3–4 inches in diameter), covered in a dense coat of fine reddish-brown to coppery velvet-soft hairs — like an incredibly large, round, red-brown cherry with velvet skin — from which the common name 'Velvet Apple' derives. Beneath the velvety skin is a firm, creamy-white to pink flesh with a sweet, mild flavor combining banana, cream, and mango notes and a dry, slightly astringent texture similar to some persimmons. The fruits have a striking, pungent odor when freshly harvested that many people find unpleasant (sometimes compared to ripe cheese or onion), but this odor dissipates with ventilation and does not affect the pleasant flavor of the flesh. In the Philippines, the tree is doubly valued: for its edible fruit and for its heartwood (called 'kamagong' — Philippine ebony), one of the hardest and heaviest woods in Southeast Asia, used for fine furniture and tool handles. The tree is also an ornamental specimen with glossy, dark-green foliage.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Diospyros blancoi (Velvet Apple)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Diospyros blancoi cultivation notes
Hawaii Cooperative Extension Service — Unusual tropical fruits: Mabolo (Velvet Apple)
International Society for Horticultural Science — Diospyros blancoi: Philippine ebony/fruit

common_name: Indonesian Wax Ginger (Pineapple Ginger, Candle Ginger, Small Taper-Lip)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Tapeinochilos ananassae cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. It performs best in the warm, humid conditions of zone 10-11 and is best treated as a container plant in zone 9. The spectacular inflorescence emerges at the base of the plant or on separate short stems from the rhizome — place where it can be viewed from ground level. The waxy bracts are exceptionally long-lasting, both on the plant and as cut flowers (2–4 weeks vase life). Divide clumps every 3 years.

directions: Plant in partial shade with consistent moisture — Tapeinochilos ananassae grows best in dappled light with regular watering and performs poorly in full sun or dry conditions. Tapeinochilos ananassae is a rhizomatous tropical perennial from Australia (Queensland) and Papua New Guinea in the Costaceae family (closely related to the spiral gingers, Costus species), producing one of the most extraordinary inflorescences in the entire ginger order. The plant grows as a clump of upright, cane-like stems clothed in spirally-arranged, glossy, dark-green leaves similar to Costus. But the inflorescence is unlike anything else in cultivation: a dense, cylindrical to conical spike of hard, waxy, overlapping, brilliant scarlet-red bracts that build upon each other to create a form strikingly and precisely resembling a miniature red pineapple — complete with the scale-like bract pattern of the pineapple's exterior. From within each bract, small, tubular yellow flowers emerge in succession. The 'pineapple ginger' inflorescence is produced on separate short flowering stems that emerge from the rhizome at ground level, distinct from the leafy canes, making the fiery red pineapple spikes appear at the plant's base surrounded by the taller leafy canes — a dramatic tropical display. The waxy bracts are exceptionally long-lasting as cut flowers (up to a month in water), making Tapeinochilos ananassae important in the tropical cut flower industry.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tapeinochilos ananassae for Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tapeinochilos ananassae cultivation notes
Heliconia Society International — Tapeinochilos species profile
Queensland Government — Tapeinochilos ananassae native range notes

common_name: Crepe Ginger (Crape Ginger, Spiral Ginger, Cane Reed)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Cheilocostus speciosus grows vigorously in zone 9-10 and can spread aggressively by rhizomes — plant in contained areas or beds where spread is acceptable. In zone 9, the canes are killed by frost but the rhizomes survive and resprout in spring with heavy mulch. Excellent for mass planting in tropical-style shade gardens under large trees. Has naturalized as invasive in parts of Florida, Louisiana, and Hawaii — check local regulations.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Crepe Ginger grows vigorously in both partial shade and full sun, making it one of the most adaptable ornamental gingers for zone 9 Gulf Coast landscapes. Cheilocostus speciosus (formerly Costus speciosus, and still widely sold under that name) is a large, rhizomatous, spreading tropical perennial native to South and Southeast Asia (India, Sri Lanka, China, Indonesia, Malaysia) in the family Costaceae, widely naturalized throughout the tropical world. The plant produces the characteristic Costus spiral stem — upright, cane-like stems up to 8 feet tall whose large, broadly elliptical, glossy, dark-green leaves are arranged in a precise clockwise or counterclockwise spiral pattern around the stem. In late summer and fall, the canes terminate in large, cone-shaped to cylindrical inflorescences of overlapping red bracts from which the true flowers emerge: large (3–4 inch), white, crepe-paper-textured petals with a yellow center — the 'crepe' quality of the petals, almost exactly resembling crinkled white crepe paper, is uniquely distinctive and gives the plant its common name. The flowers are produced successively over weeks. The large rhizomes spread aggressively and can form extensive colonies in appropriate growing conditions — the plant has naturalized in parts of Florida, Louisiana, and Hawaii. The rhizomes are used medicinally in traditional Ayurvedic medicine.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cheilocostus speciosus (Crepe Ginger)
LSU AgCenter — Ornamental Gingers for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Costus/Cheilocostus speciosus cultivation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Spiral gingers for zone 9 Gulf Coast
